preFix.title: Macramé owl wall hanging
preFix.subtitle: A 3 mm natural cotton cord owl on a wooden dowel, approximately 30 × 45 cm including fringe ears
preFix.excerpt: A macramé owl wall hanging in natural 3 mm cotton cord, worked on a wooden dowel. The body uses square knots throughout; double half-hitches form the wings and face outline; gathering knots make the eye pupils; lark's head knots mount all cords. Finished size is approximately 25 cm wide and 45 cm tall including fringe ears.
preFix.sourceNotes: Observation-led from the mid-20th-century macramé revival and the wider knotted textile tradition. Square knots, double half-hitches, and lark's head mounts are standard macramé knots documented in Graumont and Hensel, Encyclopedia of Knots and Fancy Rope Work, Cornell Maritime Press, 1939. Figurative macramé owl designs are a well-established pattern type in the revival tradition.

content.text: This macramé owl is worked from the top of the head downward on a 28 cm wooden dowel. The body is built in
square knots
; the wings and face outline are formed with
double half hitches
worked diagonally; all cords are mounted with
lark's head knots
. The fringe ear sections are added last. Finished size: approximately 25 cm wide and 45 cm tall including the fringe ears.
Cord cutting guide
Main body: cut 20 cords at 200 cm each in 3 mm natural cotton cord. Each lark's head mount produces two working ends, giving 40 ends to work with.
Ear fringe: cut 8 cords at 60 cm each. These are mounted after the body is complete and left as loose fringe to form the ears at the top corners.
Key knots used

definition.caption: Lark's head knot — fold one cord in half and mount both ends through the loop onto the dowel
Square knot — two working cords around two filler cords; forms the owl body texture
Double half hitch (left) — two loops of the working cord around the holding cord; used for diagonal wing lines

content.text: Method
Step 1: Mount cords on the dowel

Secure the dowel to your macramé board with T-pins. Mount all 20 main body cords with lark's head knots, working from the centre outward: mount 10 cords to the left of centre and 10 to the right, spacing them evenly across a 25 cm span. You now have 40 working ends hanging downward.
Step 2: Head rows

Work 4 rows of alternating square knots across all 40 cords to form the top of the owl's head. For row 1, group cords in sets of 4 and tie a square knot in each group: 10 square knots across. For row 2, offset by 2 cords: skip the first 2 cords and work square knots in groups of 4 across the remaining 36 cords, then leave the last 2 cords free. Continue alternating for rows 3 and 4.
Step 3: Eye circles

Divide the 40 cords into two groups of 20 (left and right eye sections). For each eye, work a gathering knot around the central 4 cords of each group using a separate short cord wrapped tightly 5 to 6 times and knotted securely at the back. The gathering knot pulls the surrounding cords inward to form the eye pupil shape. Pin each eye into a round shape before tightening.
Step 4: Face outline with double half hitches

Take the outermost cord on the left side as a holding cord and work double half hitches inward diagonally from left to right across the left half of the piece, angling downward to form the left side of the face frame. Repeat from the right side, working diagonally inward to meet at the centre below the eyes. Pin each row as you work to maintain the curve.
Step 5: Body

Work 6 to 8 further rows of alternating square knots below the face outline to build up the owl breast. The body section should reach approximately 15 cm below the eye row before moving to the wing shaping.
Step 6: Wing outlines

Use a holding cord diagonally from each outer edge, working double half hitches inward and downward to form the upper wing lines. A second diagonal holding cord from each side, angled back outward, forms the lower wing edge. The wing shape is created by the V formed between two diagonal lines of double half hitches on each side.
Step 7: Tail fringe

Leave the remaining cord lengths below the wing section as a loose fringe: this forms the owl tail and feather bottom edge. Trim the fringe to a V or U shape using sharp scissors. Brush the fringe ends lightly with a stiff brush to separate the fibres into a soft feathered texture.
Step 8: Ear fringe

Mount the 8 ear fringe cords (4 per ear) with lark's head knots on the dowel at each outer corner of the piece, above the main cord area. Fold each fringe cord so one end is slightly longer than the other to give the ear a pointed shape. Trim and fluff. The ears extend above the dowel level when the piece is hung.
Finishing

Weave all loose knot tails back into the work using a blunt darning needle. Trim any protruding ends flush. The piece can be sprayed lightly with water and the knot areas pressed flat with a warm iron through a damp cloth to even the texture if needed.

initialFindings.message: Tutorial PUBLISHED with heroMediaId IS NULL — hero-fill must be run
tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
